description: UI/UX Design interview questions for junior or entry-level candidates.

questions.question: Why is user research important in UX design?

choices.text: It helps designers understand user needs, behaviors, and pain points before making design decisions.
It guarantees that every design will be perfect.
It replaces the need for visual design.
It is only useful after the product is launched.

questions.correctExplanation: User research helps designers make informed decisions based on real user needs instead of assumptions.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Research improves decisions but does not guarantee perfection.
Research supports design decisions but does not replace visual design work.
Research is useful before, during, and after product development.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a wireframe?

choices.text: To explore layout, structure, and content hierarchy before focusing on detailed visual design.
To create the final production-ready visual style.
To write backend code.
To replace usability testing.

questions.correctExplanation: Wireframes help designers plan structure and user flow before investing time in polished visuals.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Wireframes are usually low or mid fidelity and are not the final visual design.
Wireframes are design artifacts, not backend code.
Wireframes can support testing, but they do not replace usability testing.

questions.question: What is the main goal of usability testing?

choices.text: To observe how users interact with a design and identify problems that affect task completion.
To prove that the designer's first idea was correct.
To make the interface more colorful.
To replace all product analytics.

questions.correctExplanation: Usability testing reveals how real users complete tasks and where they struggle.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Testing should be used to learn, not to defend an initial idea.
Color can matter, but usability testing focuses on task success and user experience.
Usability testing and analytics provide different kinds of insights.

questions.question: Which design choice usually improves accessibility?

choices.text: Using sufficient color contrast between text and background.
Using very small text to fit more content on the screen.
Relying only on color to communicate important states.
Removing labels from form fields to make the UI look cleaner.

questions.correctExplanation: Sufficient color contrast helps users read content more easily, including users with low vision.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Very small text can reduce readability.
Color alone may not be accessible to users with color vision differences.
Labels help users understand what information is required.

questions.question: How can user personas help a design team?

choices.text: They summarize key user goals, behaviors, and needs to guide design decisions.
They replace all real user research permanently.
They make every user exactly the same.
They are only used to choose colors.

questions.correctExplanation: Personas help teams stay focused on user needs and design for realistic user scenarios.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Personas should be based on research and updated as new insights appear.
Personas simplify patterns but should not erase user diversity.
Personas guide broader design decisions, not just visual style.

questions.question: What does a user flow usually show?

choices.text: The steps a user takes to complete a task within a product or service.
Only the final color palette.
The company's financial report.
A list of database tables.

questions.correctExplanation: A user flow maps the sequence of actions and decisions a user takes to reach a goal.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Color palettes are part of visual design, not user flow mapping.
Financial reports are not user flows.
Database tables may support a product but are not the same as user flows.

questions.question: What is a strong way to respond to design feedback?

choices.text: Ask clarifying questions, understand the reason behind the feedback, and decide how to improve the design.
Reject all feedback because the designer knows best.
Apply every suggestion without thinking about the user or product goal.
Take the feedback personally and stop presenting your work.

questions.correctExplanation: A good designer listens to feedback, understands the goal, and makes thoughtful decisions.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Rejecting all feedback can make collaboration difficult.
Not every suggestion should be applied; feedback should be evaluated.
Taking feedback personally can limit growth and collaboration.

questions.question: What is one major benefit of a design system?

choices.text: It helps teams create consistent interfaces using shared components, patterns, and guidelines.
It removes the need for designers and developers to communicate.
It guarantees that every product decision is correct.
It only stores images for marketing campaigns.

questions.correctExplanation: Design systems improve consistency, efficiency, and collaboration by documenting reusable UI patterns and components.

wrongExplanations.explanation: A design system supports communication but does not replace it.
Design systems improve consistency but do not guarantee every decision is correct.
Design systems are broader than image storage.

questions.question: What is visual hierarchy used for in UI design?

choices.text: To guide users' attention and help them understand what is most important on the screen.
To make every element the same size and color.
To hide important actions from users.
To replace navigation.

questions.correctExplanation: Visual hierarchy uses size, spacing, color, contrast, and placement to guide attention and improve usability.

wrongExplanations.explanation: If everything looks the same, users may not know what to focus on.
Good hierarchy helps users find important actions.
Hierarchy supports navigation but does not replace it.

questions.question: Why might a designer create a prototype before development?

choices.text: To test interactions, communicate ideas, and validate flows before building the final product.
To avoid user testing.
To replace all frontend code.
To make the design process slower without any benefit.

questions.correctExplanation: Prototypes help teams test ideas, gather feedback, and reduce risk before development.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Prototypes often make user testing easier, not unnecessary.
A prototype is not the same as production code.
Prototypes can save time by catching problems early.

questions.question: A dashboard has no data yet for a new user. What should a good empty state do?

choices.text: Explain what the user can do next and guide them toward a meaningful first action.
Show a blank page with no explanation.
Display an error message even though nothing is broken.
Hide the entire dashboard permanently.

questions.correctExplanation: A good empty state reduces confusion and helps users understand how to get value from the product.

wrongExplanations.explanation: A blank page can make users feel lost.
No data is not always an error.
Hiding the dashboard does not help users begin.

questions.question: What is an important consideration when designing for mobile screens?

choices.text: Prioritize clear content, readable text, and touch-friendly interactive elements.
Use as many tiny buttons as possible to fit more features.
Assume hover interactions will work the same as desktop.
Hide all navigation because mobile screens are small.

questions.correctExplanation: Mobile design requires readable content, clear hierarchy, and interactive elements that are comfortable to tap.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Tiny buttons can be hard to tap and reduce usability.
Hover is not reliable on touch devices.
Navigation should be simplified, not completely hidden.

questions.question: What makes a design portfolio case study stronger in an interview?

choices.text: Explaining the problem, process, decisions, trade-offs, and outcome clearly.
Showing only final screenshots with no context.
Avoiding any mention of constraints.
Claiming every decision was perfect.

questions.correctExplanation: A strong case study shows how you think, not just what the final design looked like.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Final visuals alone do not show your design reasoning.
Constraints are part of real design work and can show good judgment.
Acknowledging trade-offs and learning is more credible than claiming perfection.

questions.question: Why might UX designers care about product metrics?

choices.text: Metrics can help evaluate whether a design change improves user behavior or business outcomes.
Metrics always explain exactly why users behave a certain way.
Metrics replace all qualitative research.
Metrics are only useful for finance teams.

questions.correctExplanation: Metrics can help measure impact, but they should often be combined with qualitative insights to understand why behavior happens.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Metrics show what happened, but not always why.
Qualitative research provides context that metrics alone may not show.
Product metrics are useful for design, product, marketing, and engineering teams.

questions.question: What helps make design handoff to developers smoother?

choices.text: Clear specs, reusable components, interaction notes, and communication about edge cases.
Sending only one screenshot without explanation.
Changing the design after development without telling anyone.
Avoiding questions from developers.

questions.correctExplanation: Clear documentation and communication reduce ambiguity and help developers implement the design correctly.

wrongExplanations.explanation: A screenshot alone often lacks behavior, spacing, states, and edge cases.
Uncommunicated changes can cause confusion and rework.
Developer questions can reveal important implementation details.
